questionText: By selling 90 ball pens for ₹160, a person loses 20%. How many ball pens should be sold for ₹96 so as to have a profit of 20%?

steps.explanation: Find the Cost Price of 90 ball pens using loss of 20%.
Find the CP of 1 ball pen.
For a profit of 20%, find the required SP per pen.
Find how many pens can be sold for ₹96 at this SP.

solution.commonMistake: Using 20% loss incorrectly or not adjusting for the different total sale amount (₹96).

questionText:
A train journey from P to D by an X-express has 4 classes of fares:
- 3 tier: ₹300, 72 berths/bogie, 8 bogies
- AC-3 tier: ₹898, 64 berths/bogie, 2 bogies
- AC-2 tier: ₹1388, 45 berths/bogie, 2 bogies
- AC-first class: ₹2691, 26 berths/bogie, 1 bogie
Distance P to D = 1100 km. Running cost: AC-bogie ₹25/km, non-AC-bogie ₹10/km.
What is the approximate profit for the railways if the X-express runs at full occupancy on a particular day?

steps.explanation: Calculate total fare collected at full occupancy.
steps.formulaExpression: Total\ Fare = \sum (bogies \times berths \times fare\ per\ berth)

steps.explanation: Calculate total running cost. AC bogies = 5 (2+2+1), non-AC bogies = 8.
steps.formulaExpression: Running\ Cost = (AC\ bogies \times 25 + Non\text{-}AC\ bogies \times 10) \times distance

steps.explanation: Calculate profit = Total fare – Total running cost.

solution.shortcut: Calculate fare and cost separately; profit ≈ ₹2,57,130 ≈ ₹2,50,000.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to count AC bogies correctly (5 bogies total: 2+2+1) for running cost.

questionText: Using the same train data as Q.92, assuming full occupancy, a bogie of which class exhibits the highest profit margin?

steps.explanation: Profit margin for 3 tier = Fare – Running cost.
Profit margin for AC-3 tier.
Profit margin for AC-2 tier.
Profit margin for AC-first class.

solution.shortcut: 3 tier: ₹84,800 is the highest among all four classes.
solution.commonMistake: Comparing AC-2 tier (₹69,920) or AC-3 tier without comparing all four.

questionText: Using the same train data as Q.92, assuming full occupancy, the highest revenue for a journey from P to D will always be generated by which class?

steps.explanation: Revenue for 3 tier = 8×72×300 = 172800.

solution.shortcut: 3 tier has the most berths and bogies, giving highest total revenue despite lower per-berth fare.
solution.commonMistake: Assuming higher fare per berth means higher total revenue.

formulas.title: Revenue
formulas.expression: Revenue = bogies \times berths\ per\ bogie \times fare

questionText: Using the same train data as Q.92, assuming full occupancy in all the classes, for a journey between P and D, the profit margin (as a percentage of running costs) of the class showing the lowest profit is approximately:

steps.explanation: From Q.93, AC-first class shows the lowest profit margin of ₹42,466. Its running cost = 1×25×1100 = ₹27,500.
steps.calculation: Running cost of AC-first class = 27500; Profit = 42466

steps.explanation: Profit as % of running cost.
steps.result: ≈ 154.4%, which is none of the given options (a), (b), (c)

solution.shortcut: AC-first class has lowest profit; 42466/27500 × 100 ≈ 154%, so answer is (d).
solution.commonMistake: Picking a wrong class as lowest profit or computing % over revenue instead of running cost.

questionText: Using the same train data as Q.92, for that full-occupancy run, the percentage of the total profit that comes out of AC bogies is approximately:

steps.explanation: Total profit from Q.92 = ₹2,57,130.
Profit from AC bogies = Revenue from AC classes – Running cost of AC bogies.
% of total profit from AC bogies.

solution.commonMistake: Including non-AC bogies in the AC profit calculation.

questionText:
A shopkeeper bought three varieties A, B and C of rice at ₹34.50, ₹28.60 and ₹32.40 per kg respectively. In which of the following transactions will he gain maximum?
(a) 25 kg C at ₹42/kg and 30 kg A at ₹38/kg
(b) 40 kg B at ₹37/kg and 20 kg A at ₹40/kg
(c) 20 kg A and 20 kg C at ₹38 and ₹36/kg respectively
(d) 30 kg A at ₹37/kg and 20 kg B at ₹33/kg
(e) 20 kg B at ₹40/kg and 40 kg C at ₹38/kg

steps.explanation: Calculate profit for option (a): 25 kg C and 30 kg A.
Option (b): 40 kg B and 20 kg A.
Options (c) and (d): both yield ₹142 and ₹163 respectively.
Option (e): 20 kg B and 40 kg C.

solution.shortcut: Compare profits for all options; option (e) gives ₹452 which is the highest.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing the variety costs or quantities between options.

questionText: Nikita bought 30 kg of wheat at ₹9.50/kg and 40 kg of wheat at ₹8.50/kg and mixed them. She sold the mixture at ₹8.90/kg. Her total profit or loss in the transaction was:

steps.explanation: Calculate total Cost Price.
Calculate total Selling Price.
Determine profit or loss.

solution.commonMistake: Calculating average price incorrectly or mixing up CP and SP.

questionText: Manish purchased 25 kg of rice @ ₹32/kg and 15 kg of rice @ ₹36/kg. He mixed the two varieties and sold it @ ₹40.20/kg. What is the percent profit earned?

steps.explanation: Calculate total Cost Price of 40 kg rice.
Calculate total Selling Price.
Calculate Profit%.

solution.commonMistake: Taking total quantity as 25+15=40 but then using a wrong SP.

questionText: One variety of sugar is sold for ₹3.20/kg at a loss of 20% and another variety is sold for ₹6/kg at a gain of 20%. If equal quantities of the two are mixed and the mixture is sold at ₹5.40/kg, what is the loss or gain percentage?

steps.explanation: Find CP of first variety sold at ₹3.20 with 20% loss.
Find CP of second variety sold at ₹6 with 20% gain.
Average CP of mixture (equal quantities mixed).
Mixture sold at ₹5.40/kg; calculate profit%.

solution.commonMistake: Using SP averages instead of CP averages for the mixture.

questionText: Arun purchased 30 kg of wheat at ₹11.50/kg and 20 kg of wheat at ₹14.25/kg. He mixed the two and sold the mixture. Approximately what price per kg should he sell the mixture to make 30% profit?

steps.explanation: Calculate total CP of 50 kg wheat.
Required SP for 30% profit.
Selling price per kg.

solution.commonMistake: Computing average CP per kg first and then applying profit, rounding midway.

questionText: Padam purchased 30 kg of rice at ₹17.50/kg and another 30 kg rice at a certain rate. He mixed the two and sold the entire quantity at ₹18.60/kg and made 20% overall profit. At what price per kg did he purchase the other 30 kg rice?

steps.explanation: Let the rate of other 30 kg be ₹x/kg. Total CP = 30×17.50 + 30×x = 525 + 30x.
Apply 20% profit condition: SP = 1.2 × CP.

solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to use 20% profit as SP = 1.2 × CP rather than (SP–CP)/SP.

questionText: A person blends two varieties of tea – one costing ₹160/kg and the other costing ₹200/kg in the ratio 5:4. He sells the blended variety at ₹192/kg. His profit percent is:

steps.explanation: Assume 5 kg and 4 kg of the two varieties.

solution.commonMistake: Using simple average of the two CPs without weighting by ratio.

questionText: A trader mixes three varieties of groundnuts costing ₹50, ₹20 and ₹30 per kg in the ratio 2:4:3 by weight, and sells the mixture at ₹33/kg. What percentage of profit does he make?

steps.explanation: Assume 2 kg, 4 kg and 3 kg of the three varieties (ratio 2:4:3).
Calculate profit%.

solution.commonMistake: Using wrong ratio or adding costs without multiplying by respective quantities.

questionText: A shopkeeper bought 30 kg of wheat at ₹45/kg. He sold 40% of the total quantity at ₹50/kg. Approximately at what price per kg should he sell the remaining quantity to make 25% overall profit?

steps.explanation: 40% of 30 kg = 12 kg sold at ₹50; SP from this = 12×50 = ₹600.
Remaining SP needed = 1687.50 – 600 = ₹1087.50 from 18 kg.

solution.shortcut: Required total SP=₹1687.50; already earned ₹600; remaining 18 kg → ₹1087.50/18 ≈ ₹60.
solution.commonMistake: Calculating 60% remaining as 60% of 30 = 18 kg (correct) but using wrong total SP.

questionText: A dealer buys dry fruit at ₹100, ₹80 and ₹60/kg in ratio 12:15:20 by weight. He gets 20% profit by selling the first two and then finds no gain no loss on selling the whole quantity. What was the percentage loss he suffered for the third quantity?

steps.explanation: No overall profit/loss means total SP = ₹3600. SP from first two at 20% profit: 120% of (1200+1200) = 120% of 2400 = ₹2880.
SP from third quantity = 3600 – 2880 = ₹720. CP of third = ₹1200.

solution.commonMistake: Not separating the SP of first two varieties from total SP.

questionText: A dairyman pays ₹6.40/litre of milk. He adds water and sells the mixture at ₹8/litre, thereby making 37.5% profit. The proportion of water to milk received by the customers is:

steps.explanation: Mean cost price of mixture to earn 37.5% profit at SP=₹8.
By alligation: CP of milk = ₹6.40 = 64/10, CP of water = 0. Mean CP = 64/11.
steps.formula: Alligation: ratio = (Mean CP – CP water) : (CP milk – Mean CP)

solution.commonMistake: Forgetting that water costs ₹0 in alligation.

questionText: Five litres of water is added to a certain quantity of pure milk costing ₹3/litre. If by selling the mixture at the same price as before, a profit of 20% is made, then what is the amount of pure milk in the mixture?

steps.explanation: Let pure milk = m litres. CP = 3m. Mixture = (m+5) litres sold at ₹3/litre.
For 20% profit: SP = 1.2 × CP.

solution.commonMistake: Mixing up the SP formula or not recognising water adds volume but no cost.

questionText: By mixing two brands of tea and selling the mixture at ₹177/kg, a shopkeeper makes a profit of 18%. If to every 2 kg of one brand costing ₹200/kg, 3 kg of the other brand is added, then how much per kg does the other brand cost?

steps.explanation: Let cost of other brand = ₹x/kg. CP of 5 kg = 2×200 + 3×x = 400 + 3x.
Profit 18%: SP = 1.18 × CP.
steps.result: ≈ ₹116.67, which is none of the given options

solution.commonMistake: Rounding ₹116.67 to ₹120 — the exact answer is 350/3 which matches 'None of these'.

questionText: The manufacturer of a certain item can sell all he can produce at ₹60 each. It costs him ₹40 in materials and labour to produce each item and he has overhead expenses of ₹3000/week. The number of units he should produce and sell in order to make a profit of at least ₹1000/week is:

steps.explanation: Profit per unit = SP – Variable CP = 60 – 40 = ₹20.
steps.formulaExpression: Profit\ per\ unit = SP - Variable\ Cost

steps.explanation: Total profit = 20n – 3000, where n = units sold. For at least ₹1000 profit.

solution.shortcut: Net profit per unit = ₹20; need ₹4000 total (overhead+target) → 4000/20 = 200 units.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to add overhead and target profit before dividing by per-unit profit.

questionText: A milkman cheats his customer in two ways: he mixes 10% water in pure milk and increases the price of milk by 10%. He purchases 20 kg pure milk at ₹15/kg. His total profit by selling it is:

steps.explanation: CP of 20 kg milk = 20 × 15 = ₹300.
Water added = 10% of 20 kg = 2 kg. Total mixture = 22 kg.

solution.commonMistake: Adding water volume without adjusting the new selling price, or computing profit as % not absolute.

questionText: A dishonest dealer uses a scale of 90 cm instead of a metre scale and claims to sell at cost price. His profit is:

steps.explanation: He gives only 90 cm but charges for 100 cm. Let CP per cm = ₹1. CP of 90 cm = ₹90; he charges SP = ₹100.

solution.commonMistake: Confusing with 10% (which would apply if SP were 100 and he gives 90 units at equal price — but here measurement is in length).

questionText: A dealer professes to sell his goods at cost price but uses a false weight of 950 grams for a kilogram. The gain percent of the dealer is:

steps.explanation: Dealer uses 950 g but charges for 1000 g. Let CP per gram = ₹1.

solution.commonMistake: Taking error over 1000 g (numerator base) instead of 950 g.

questionText: A fruit seller professes to sell his fruits at cost price, but still gains 25% on his outlay. What weight does he substitute for a kilogram?

steps.explanation: Let the false weight be x grams. He charges for 1000 g but gives x g.

solution.commonMistake: Using (1000–x)/1000 instead of (1000–x)/x for gain%.

questionText: A dishonest dealer professes to sell his goods at cost price but uses a false weight and thus gains $\frac{6}{47}\%$ (i.e., $\frac{6}{47} \times 100\% = $ approximately). [Note: The gain is $\frac{600}{47}\%$. For a kg, he uses a weight of:]

steps.explanation: The gain percent given in the text is $\frac{6}{47}\%$ — interpreted as the fractional gain percentage. Using the formula: False weight = 1000 × 100 / (100 + Gain%).
steps.calculation: If Gain% = 6000/47 ÷ 100 = 60/47 is ambiguous; the answer key gives (a) 940 gms. Check: gain on 940g = (1000-940)/940 × 100 = 60/940×100 = 6000/940 = 600/94 ≈ 6.38%. Alternatively if gain is 6+18/47 % → false wt ≈ 940.

solution.shortcut: False weight = 1000×100/(100 + Gain%); with the given gain, false weight = 940 gm.
solution.commonMistake: Misreading the gain percentage from the garbled text; always verify by back-calculating.

questionText: A shopkeeper cheats to the extent of 10% while buying as well as selling, by using false weights. His total gain is:

steps.explanation: When cheating at 10% while buying: for ₹100 worth, he gets 110 units (gets extra 10%).
steps.calculation: He buys 110 units at cost of 100 units

steps.explanation: When cheating at 10% while selling: charges for 100 units but gives only 90 units.
steps.calculation: SP for 100 units = ₹100 (at CP); but gives only 90 → effectively sells 110 units for SP of (110/90)×100

steps.formula: Gain% when cheating x% on both sides = (100+x)²/100 – 100

solution.commonMistake: Adding 10+10=20% instead of using the compound formula.

questionText: Left pan of a faulty balance weighs 100 grams more than its right pan. A shopkeeper keeps the weight measure in the left pan while buying and in the right pan while selling. He uses only a 1 kg weight measure. If he sells at listed cost price, what is his gain?

steps.explanation: While buying: 1 kg weight is in the heavier left pan. Left pan is 100g heavier. So to balance, goods in right pan = 1000 + 100 = 1100 g. He buys 1100g for the price of 1000g.
While selling: 1 kg weight is in the lighter right pan. Right pan is 100g lighter. So to balance, goods in left pan = 1000 – 100 = 900 g. He sells only 900g but charges for 1000g.

solution.shortcut: Buy 1100g for price of 1000g; sell 900g for price of 1000g → effective gain = (1100–900)/900 × 100 = 200/9%.
solution.commonMistake: Not accounting for both buying and selling distortions of the faulty balance.

questionText: A grocer sells rice at a profit of 10% and uses weights which are 20% less than the market weight. The total gain earned by him will be:

steps.explanation: Actual weight given = 80% of market weight = 800g per kg. Let CP per gram = ₹1. CP of packet = ₹800.
SP = 110% of CP of true 1 kg = 110% of ₹1000 = ₹1100.

solution.shortcut: Formula: Gain% = (x + y + xy/100)% where x=10% profit on SP, y=20% weight reduction → using actual formula: (10+20+10×20/100)% would give 32% which is wrong. Correct: Gain% = (100+x)/(100-y) × 100 – 100 = 110/80×100–100 = 37.5%.
solution.commonMistake: Using simple addition of 10+20 = 30% instead of compound formula.

formulas.title: Gain% with false weight and profit%

questionText: A dishonest dealer sells the goods at 20% loss on cost price but uses 15% less weight. What is his percentage profit or loss?

steps.explanation: Let CP = ₹100 per kg true weight. He sells at 20% loss: SP = ₹80 per nominal kg.
He gives only 85% of weight: actual weight = 850g per kg. CP of 850g = ₹85.

solution.shortcut: Formula: Gain/Loss% = (y – x)/(100 – y) × 100 where x = loss% = 20, y = weight reduction% = 15 → (15–20)/85 × 100 = –5/85×100 = –100/17% loss.
solution.commonMistake: Treating 20% loss and 15% weight reduction as additive: –20+15 = –5%.

questionText: A merchant professes to lose 4% on a certain tea but uses a weight equal to 840 g instead of 1 kg. Find his real loss or gain percent.

steps.explanation: Weight deduction = 1000 – 840 = 160 g; percentage deduction = 160/1000 × 100 = 16%.
He also professes to lose 4% on price: x = –4 (it is a loss on price side). Using formula: Gain/Loss% = (y – x)/(100 – y) × 100.

solution.shortcut: CP of 840g = ₹84 (base ₹100/kg); SP at 4% loss on nominal kg = ₹96; gain = ₹12 on ₹84 → 12/84×100 = 100/7 = 14 2/7%.
solution.commonMistake: Applying the formula with the wrong sign for the professed loss.

formulas.title: Gain% with false weight and price adjustment
formulas.expression: Gain\% = \frac{SP - CP\ of\ actual\ weight}{CP\ of\ actual\ weight} \times 100

questionText: A shopkeeper advertises for selling cloth at 4% loss. However, by using a false metre scale he actually gains 20%. What is the actual length of the scale?

steps.explanation: Let the false scale give y% less than 1 metre. Gain/Loss% = (y – x)/(100 – y) × 100, where x = 4% (loss on price) treated as –4% gain.

Re-derive: Let percentage deduction in length = y%. Then using (y–x)/(100–y)×100 = 20 where x = –4: (y+4)/(100–y) = 20/100 = 1/5 → 5y+20 = 100–y → 6y = 80 → y = 80/6 = 13.33? That gives scale = 86.67 cm. Better: directly set up. Let scale = L cm. CP per cm = ₹1. He sells L cm at price of 100 cm (professes to sell at CP of 1m). Professed SP = ₹100. Actual SP (with 4% loss on professed CP of 1m) = 96. CP of L cm = L.

solution.shortcut: Actual scale L: SP=₹96 (4% less than ₹100 CP of 1m), actual CP=L; 20% gain → 96=1.2L → L=80 cm.
solution.commonMistake: Using the loss percent formula incorrectly when the dealer professes to lose.

questionText: Instead of a metre scale, a cloth merchant uses a 120 cm scale while buying but uses an 80 cm scale while selling the same cloth. If he offers a discount of 20% on cash payment, what is his overall profit percentage?

steps.explanation: Let actual CP of 1 m cloth = ₹1. When buying: he uses 120 cm scale, so pays ₹1 for 120 cm of cloth. CP per cm = 1/120.
When selling: he uses 80 cm scale, charges ₹1 per scale (for 80 cm). He offers 20% discount, so SP = ₹0.80 per 80 cm = ₹1 per metre (listed) × 0.80.

solution.commonMistake: Not applying the 20% discount on the selling price.

questionText: A trader professes to sell his goods at a nominal gain percentage but actually earns $37\frac{1}{2}\%$ profit by using a false weight. If for a kg he uses a weight of 800 gm, what is the nominal gain percentage at which he claims to be selling his goods?

steps.explanation: Weight deduction = 200g on 1000g; percentage deduction = 200/1000×100 = 20%.
Actual gain = 37.5%. Using: Actual Gain% = (x + y + xy/100)% where x = nominal gain, y = weight reduction = 20%.

solution.shortcut: Formula: (nominal gain% + weight reduction%) / (100 – weight reduction%) × 100 = actual gain%; (x+20)/80 × 100 = 37.5 → x = 10.
solution.commonMistake: Using (x+y+xy/100) formula without accounting for the base correctly.

formulas.title: Actual gain% from nominal and weight reduction

questionText: A dry fruit merchant professes to sell 2 kg almond packs at a loss of 20%. However, he uses two false weights each marked 1 kg and thus gains $6\frac{2}{3}\%$ on selling every 2 kg of almonds. If one of the weights weighs only 850 gm, then how much does the other weight weigh?

steps.explanation: Let CP of 1 kg almonds = ₹100. SP (at 20% loss) = ₹80 per nominal kg; SP for 2 kg pack = ₹160.

steps.calculation: CP of actual weight delivered = ₹150 → actual weight delivered = 150/100 × 1000 = 1500 gm

steps.explanation: One false weight = 850 gm. Other false weight = 1500 – 850 = 650 gm.

solution.shortcut: Total actual weight = 1500g (= CP₁₅₀₀g at ₹100/kg); one weight = 850g; other = 650g.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting that the gain applies to the actual quantity delivered, not the nominal 2 kg.

formulas.title: Actual CP from SP and Gain%

questionText:
A stockist wants to make some profit by selling sugar. Which of the following methods would maximize his profit?
(a) Sell sugar at 10% profit
(b) Use 900 g of weight instead of 1 kg
(c) Mix 10% impurities in sugar and sell sugar at cost price
(d) Increase the price by 5% and reduce the weight by 5%
options: B) Use 900g weight instead of 1 kg
C) Mix 10% impurities and sell at CP
D) Increase price by 5% and reduce weight by 5%
correctAnswer: B) Use 900g weight instead of 1 kg

steps.explanation: Option (a): Profit = 10%.
Option (c): Mix 10% impurities, sell 1.1 kg quantity for price of 1 kg. SP = CP, but actual quantity per unit cost = 1.1 kg. Profit = 10%.
Option (d): Price up 5%, weight down 5%. Effective gain% = [(1.05)/(0.95) – 1] × 100 ≈ 10.5%. Let CP=₹1/kg, actual CP=₹0.95, SP=₹1.05. Gain% = 0.10/0.95×100 = 10.53%.
Compare: (a)=10%, (b)=11.11%, (c)=10%, (d)=10.53%. Maximum is option (b).

solution.commonMistake: Assuming option (d) gives more profit than (b) without computing exactly.

questionText: A fair price shopkeeper takes 10% profit on his goods. He lost 20% goods during theft. His loss percent is:

steps.explanation: Assume he has 100 items at CP = ₹1 each. Total CP = ₹100. After theft, he has 80 items.
SP per item = ₹1.10 (10% profit). Total SP = 80 × 1.10 = ₹88.

solution.shortcut: 100 items at ₹1 each; sell 80 at ₹1.10; total SP=₹88; loss=₹12 on ₹100 → 12%.
solution.commonMistake: Computing on 80 items instead of all 100 as the base CP.

questionText: Prateek sold a music system to Kartik at 20% gain and Kartik sold it to Swastik at 40% gain. If Swastik paid ₹10500 for the music system, what amount did Prateek pay for the same?

steps.explanation: Let Prateek's CP = ₹x. Kartik's CP = 120% of x = 1.2x. Swastik's CP = 140% of 1.2x = 1.68x.
Solve for x.

solution.commonMistake: Adding 20+40 = 60% gain instead of compounding: 1.2 × 1.4 = 1.68.

questionText: A manufacturer sells an article to a wholesale dealer at a profit of 20% and the wholesale dealer sells it to a retail merchant at a loss of 5%. Find the resultant loss or profit.

steps.explanation: Let manufacturer's CP = ₹100. Wholesale dealer's CP = 120% of 100 = ₹120.
Retail merchant's CP = 95% of 120 = ₹114.
Resultant gain from manufacturer's CP perspective = (114–100)/100 × 100 = 14%.

solution.commonMistake: Subtracting 20–5 = 15% instead of using compounding.

questionText: A bought a radio set and spent ₹110 on its repairs. He then sold it to B at 20% profit, B sold it to C at a loss of 10% and C sold it for ₹1188 at a profit of 10%. What is the amount for which A bought the radio set?

steps.explanation: Work backwards from C's sale: C sold at ₹1188 at 10% profit. C's CP = 1188/1.10 = 1080.
B sold to C at 10% loss: B's SP = C's CP = 1080. B's CP = 1080/0.90 = 1200.
A sold to B at 20% profit: A's SP = 1200. A's effective CP (including repairs) = 1200/1.20 = 1000.
A spent ₹110 on repairs; so A's actual purchase price = 1000 – 110 = ₹890.

solution.commonMistake: Not subtracting ₹110 repair cost from total outlay, or chaining multiplications in wrong order.

questionText: A car worth ₹1,50,000 was sold by X to Y at 5% profit. Y sold the car back to X at 2% loss. In the entire transaction:

steps.explanation: X sells car to Y at 5% profit: SP = 1,50,000 × 1.05 = ₹1,57,500.
Y sells car back to X at 2% loss: Y's SP = 1,57,500 × 0.98 = ₹1,54,350.
X's net gain: received ₹1,57,500 – paid ₹1,54,350 = ₹3,150.

solution.commonMistake: Confusing who gains and who loses, or using ₹1,50,000 as Y's CP for the second transaction.

questionText: A manufacturer sold a machine to a wholesale dealer at a profit of 10%. The wholesale dealer sold it to a retailer at a profit of 20%. While transporting, some defect occurred and hence the retailer sold it at a loss of 5%. The customer paid ₹627. Find the cost of the machine for the manufacturer.

steps.explanation: Let manufacturer's CP = ₹x. Chain: 110% × 120% × 95% of x = 627.

solution.commonMistake: Compounding the percentages additively (10+20–5=25%) instead of multiplicatively.

questionText: Goods pass successively through the hands of three traders and each of them sells his goods at a profit of 25% of his cost price. If the last trader sold the goods for ₹250, then how much did the first trader pay for them?

steps.explanation: Let first trader's CP = ₹x. Three successive 25% profits: 125% × 125% × 125% of x = 250.

solution.commonMistake: Adding 25×3 = 75% instead of using compound profit.

questionText: An article passing through two hands is sold at a profit of 38% at the original cost price. If the first dealer makes a profit of 20%, then the profit percent made by the second is:

steps.explanation: Let original CP = ₹100. Final SP = ₹138 (38% overall profit). First dealer sells at 20% profit: 1st dealer's SP = ₹120.

solution.commonMistake: Computing (138–120)/100 instead of /120.

formulas.title: 2nd dealer profit%
formulas.expression: Profit\% = \frac{Final\ SP - 1st\ dealer's\ SP}{1st\ dealer's\ SP} \times 100

questionText: A merchant buys an article for ₹27 and sells it at a profit of 10 percent of the selling price. The selling price of the article is:

steps.explanation: Let SP = ₹x. Profit = 10% of SP = 0.1x. CP = SP – Profit = x – 0.1x = 0.9x.

solution.shortcut: Profit on SP not CP: SP – 10%SP = CP → 90%SP = 27 → SP = 30.
solution.commonMistake: Computing 10% on CP instead of SP: 27×1.10 = 29.70 (wrong).

formulas.title: SP when profit% is on SP
